feedback.text: I would like to express my strong support for the goals of the Digital Fairness Act, but I urge you to include a critical and growing issue affecting millions of consumers across Europe: the ability of game publishers to unilaterally terminate access to digital games that users have legally purchased. A recent personal example illustrates the problem clearly. I purchased The Crew, published by the French company Ubisoft. Just one month later, I was informed that I would permanently lose access to the game. No reimbursement was offered, and no alternative was provided to continue playing the product I had legally bought. This is not an isolated case. Hundreds of games have already been rendered inaccessible to consumers in similar ways, and many more are at risk of becoming unusable in the near future. Digital products should not be treated as disposable. When consumers pay for a game, they should not lose access simply because the publisher decides to withdraw support. At the very least, companies should be required to ensure that a game remains playable in some for, such as through offline modes or patches at the end of support so users can continue using the products they purchased. I am a supporter of the European Citizens Initiative Stop Killing Games, which has gathered over 1.4 million signatures. This shows that there is strong public demand for better consumer protections in this area. I respectfully ask that you address these concerns in the final version of the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you for your consideration. Sincerely, David Blaha Germany
